title: Lesson 228
transcript: As part of security management, Security Forever has been saying that security is everyone's responsibility and that's certainly true. Now what's important for security to be effective, however, is not only to say that message to everyone in the organization, but that message really should be communicated by upper management. And the failure that we've seen in security over the last number of decades, for example, typically has been because that message that security is everyone's responsibility, has actually come from security, and that is bound to fail. The message itself has to come from upper management. In fact, it would be nice if it came from the board of directors, and the person that is acting on behalf of the board of directors, which of course is the CEO. But imagine how much weight that would have and the culture and the tone that would be created, for example, if the CEO said on a regular basis that security is everyone's responsibility. Now we know that that's true. However, it's very important for us to understand the difference between two very important words that are often misused and interchanged in security. And those two words are actually the words accountability and responsibility. So these two words are often used interchangeably when they really shouldn't because the two words responsibility and accountability are not the same, especially in how we use them related to security. Like we've said, you know, security is everyone's responsibility and that message needs to come from the top. However, those that are accountable need to remain accountable. In fact, still remain accountable. That's the difference between these two words. responsibility and accountability. In fact, when you look those up, if you are accountable for something you can never delegate that accountability to anyone else. You will always remain accountable. And so if you look at what security does for any organization, it contributes towards its goals and objectives that are always focused on increasing value. There are people in the organization that are accountable for that. and that would be the board of directors and the person acting on behalf of the board of directors who is the ceo so the ceo is really accountable for increasing the value of the organization i mean the ceo has only one statement in their job description And that is to increase the value of the organization and its assets. Security must contribute towards that. So while we say security is everyone's responsibility, It really remains the accountability of the CEO to take care of that requirement that security really contributes towards increasing value. And so when you look at the two words, it's pretty important for us to understand the difference and use them correctly in the right context. So accountability is something that you can never delegate to anyone else. You can delegate the responsibility but you remain accountable. And so what might be really important for us to remember as far as the exam is concerned is how these words apply to some really important functions in the organization related to security. So if you look at, for example, executive management and the CEO, for example, and the board of directors, they would always be accountable. That's the word that you associate with. that group or that role within the organization. Us, and we are referred to information systems security professionals. In other words, the security function. We would be responsible for ensuring, for example, that the right controls are in place, that we are implementing, for example, effective, efficient, cost-effective controls in the organization so that ultimately we are aligned with goals and objectives. So in this group of people that you see here in this slide, for example, most of them are going to be responsible, but there are some very important groups like executive management and the owners, for example, the owners are going to be accountable. for protecting what they own. Obviously assets in the organization that represent value to the organization itself. And custodians, that word custodian comes from the word custody. So you have custody of something that doesn't necessarily belong to you. It belongs to an owner somewhere else. And so therefore, you would also be responsible for protecting those assets that you have access to while they're in your custody. So they remain, for example, the accountability of the owner, but you as a custodian. a user of that asset would be responsible for protecting it. So users and custodians are typically the same group of people. They are responsible for securing what they have custody of or what they're using for an extended period of time that obviously owns or is owned by someone else. And auditors, very important function in the organization. They may ask you questions related to what auditors provide in the organization. And if we were to summarize that, it would be the word assurance. Auditors provide assurance to those that are accountable our stakeholders, for example, not only upper management, the CEO, the board of directors, but your stakeholders could be also external, like compliance requirements for regulators, Or your clients, your customers, your shareholders, for example. They want to know that we've got the right levels of security and that security is contributing towards value as well. And so auditors provide a very important function related to security being assurance and confidence. Now, how would you answer this question, which is obviously very important for us to focus on? Who is ultimately accountable for security and therefore protecting all of those valuable assets? in the organization would be any of these answers that you see here, but it should be in the order as this that I'm going to highlight for you. First of all, the board of directors. and then the CEO that is acting on behalf of the board of directors and then of course the CEO may you know structure executive management to take care of those accountabilities so any of these groups if they're in the list of answers you would pick for being accountable Now this question should be easy for us to answer. Who is specifically responsible for security? And this is what we've been saying for decades and that is everyone in the organization.
